attributes.stem:
A 78-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with a three-day history of decreased urine output and increasing abdominal distension. He has a longstanding history of alcohol-related cirrhosis, complicated by recurrent ascites and hepatic encephalopathy. His other comorbidities are alcohol-induced cardiomyopathy and moderate cognitive impairment. His regular medications include spironolactone, furosemide, carvedilol, lactulose and thiamine. He continues to drink approximately four standard drinks of alcohol per day.

On examination, he appears frail and unwell with a pulse rate of 110/min, blood pressure 95/60 mmHg, respiratory rate 22/min, SpO2 96% on room air, and temperature 37.2°C. His abdomen is distended with shifting dullness. His chest is clear on auscultation and there is bilateral pitting oedema to the knees. Investigations reveal:

  • Serum creatinine: 380 µmol/L (60-110)

  • Serum sodium: 132 mmol/L (135-145)

  • Serum potassium: 5.2 mmol/L (3.5-5.0)

  • Serum albumin: 23 g/L (35-50)

  • Total bilirubin: 65 µmol/L (3-20)

  • ALT: 45 U/L (5-40)

  • AST: 60 U/L (5-40)

  • INR: 2.5

Despite careful fluid rehydration and suspension of his diuretics, his renal function worsens over the next week. No other reversible cause of his deterioration is identified.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features suggestive of hepatorenal syndrome (HRS), a form of acute kidney injury (AKI) that occurs in patients with advanced liver disease. HRS is characterized by renal vasoconstriction in the setting of systemic and splanchnic vasodilation due to portal hypertension. A key diagnostic feature of HRS is a very low urinary sodium concentration (typically <10 mmol/L) due to avid sodium retention. HRS is important to consider in cirrhotic patients who fail to respond to volume expansion and diuretic withdrawal in the absence of other causes of AKI.

Supportive care is the most appropriate next step in this scenario. Given the patient's advanced age, ongoing alcohol use, the severity of his liver disease and other comorbidities the patient's overall prognosis is very poor, and the focus should shift towards symptom management and end-of-life planning. This approach prioritises the patient's comfort and quality of life.

Terlipressin with albumin is a specific treatment for HRS. Terlipressin is a vasopressin analogue that causes systemic vasoconstriction, which helps to redistribute blood flow to the kidneys. It's primarily used as a bridge to liver transplantation, aiming to improve renal function temporarily. However, in this case, the patient would not be a suitable transplant candidate due to his ongoing alcohol use and advanced age. Therefore, initiating terlipressin would not be appropriate as it carries risks without offering a long-term solution.

Haemodialysis or peritoneal dialysis would not be appropriate in this context. Given the patient's poor prognosis due to advanced liver disease and other comorbidities, initiating dialysis is unlikely to provide significant benefit and may cause unnecessary discomfort. The risks and burdens of dialysis likely outweigh any potential short-term benefits in this scenario.

Beginning a liver transplant workup is not appropriate for this patient. His ongoing alcohol use and advanced age make him an unsuitable candidate for transplantation. Additionally, his overall poor prognosis suggests that focusing on comfort care and symptom management would be more beneficial than pursuing aggressive interventions.

In summary, given the patient's overall clinical picture, the most appropriate approach is to focus on supportive care, symptom management, and discussions about goals of care and end-of-life planning with the patient and his family.
learningPoints: Hepatorenal syndrome is an important cause of acute kidney injury in patients with cirrhosis and acute kidney injury, with liver transplantation being the only curative therapy.
While terlipressin is an effective therapy for hepatorenal syndrome, it is considered a bridge to transplantation.
In patients with advanced liver disease who are not transplant candidates, the focus of care may shift to symptom management and end-of-life planning.
references: Pose E, Piano S, Juanola A, Ginès P. Hepatorenal Syndrome in Cirrhosis. Gastroenterology. 2024 Apr;166(4):588-604.e1. Available at: https://doi.org/10.1053/j.gastro.2023.11.306
Francoz C, Durand F, Kahn JA, Genyk YS, Nadim MK. Hepatorenal Syndrome. Clin J Am Soc Nephrol. 2019 May 7;14(5):774-781. Available at: https://doi.org/10.2215/CJN.12451018
